title: Sister Vess
paragraphs:
The woman who was coming to kill the prophet did not yet know his name, only the shape of the problem.

She read the report again by candlelight in a wayside inn called The Broken Hasp, two days' ride from Aldmoor. The page was damp at the edges. All paper was damp now. The paper and the bread and the inside of her boots and the back of her teeth.

Sister Vess had been a Seeker for the Crown Church for seventeen years. Before that she had been a girl in a fishing village, and before that she had been, for a brief and uncomplicated stretch of months, a baby. She did not think about the girl or the baby very often. She thought about the Seeker.

The report said: Aldmoor, Prefecture of Graywater. Unusual religious gathering. Unsanctioned ministry. Reports of ecstatic states and unaccounted-for physical phenomena. Two missing persons in the adjacent parish, no bodies recovered. Father Aulen, incumbent priest, has not answered four consecutive courier runs. Investigate. Intercede. Report.

Intercede.

The word did a great deal of work in Seeker correspondence. It meant, at the softest end, a long letter. At the hard end, it meant a woman like Sister Vess stepping out from behind a rain-wet tree and putting a thin, gently curved knife into the base of someone's skull while they were in the middle of a very important thought.

The innkeeper was a wide, sad woman named Brusa who poured the small beer with a practiced wrist and did not make conversation. Vess liked her for it.

Two men sat at the far end of the common room. A wool merchant and his guard, Vess guessed, from the cut of their coats and the way the guard sat with his back to the wall and his right hand loose in his lap. They had been arguing quietly for twenty minutes, in the rhythm of men who had done this argument many times before.

"It's the rain," the merchant said. "The wool won't take dye, so the dye-master won't take the wool, so we eat it and smile. That's all it is."

"It isn't the rain," said the guard.

"What, then."

"I don't know what."

After another silence, the guard said, "In Wendover they're saying a saint came out of the fen."

The merchant snorted. "A saint."

"That's what they're saying. Heals the sick. Walks on the wet without sinking."

"And what does your saint want?"

The guard took a long time to answer. When he did, his voice had gone thin. "They say he wants nothing. That's the part that bothers me. A man who wants nothing. I've met a lot of men. I never met one of those."

The merchant was about to reply when the inn door opened and a great deal of rain and a tall thin shape came in together.

The shape resolved itself into a young man, or a very beautiful boy, no older than nineteen. He was soaking wet. He was not wearing a coat. His feet were bare, and blue-white, and the floor under them began at once to pool. He looked around the room with an expression of untroubled interest, like a well-bred guest at a party.

He walked to the fire. He did not warm his hands. He stood with his back to it, so that the light burned a line around the edges of his hair, and he smiled.
"Blessings on this house," he said. "I am sent to tell the woman in the corner that she is expected."

Nobody moved. Then, slowly, every head in the room turned to Sister Vess.

She did not turn in her chair. She closed the report and folded it and put it inside her coat against her ribs, where it would be warm and would not rustle. She took one slow sip of the small beer. Then she stood, and the bench scraped, and she crossed the room toward the fire.

Up close the boy smelled of wet wood and of something sweet going bad.

"What is your name," Vess said. It was not a question.

"I was called Finn once."

"And now?"

The boy's smile did not change. "Now I am called nothing, because there is no one in me to answer to it. The Shepherd sends his peace. He says: come to Aldmoor, sister. The door is open. The kettle is on. Do not be afraid."
"I am not afraid," said Sister Vess, which was, for the first time in her seventeen years of service, a lie.

The boy called Finn turned and walked back out into the rain without touching the door. It opened for him and closed behind him and Brusa the innkeeper, who had seen a great many things in her forty-one years, put down the pitcher of small beer very carefully, as if it were full of something else, and sat down on the floor and began, quietly, to cry.
Sister Vess did not comfort her. She went upstairs and checked her knives.
